questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Three of the four reactions shown below proceed as shown to give good yields. Identify the one reaction that will not proceed as indicated.
questions.solution:
The relative reactivity of carboxylic acid derivatives towards nucleophilic acyl substitution follows the order: Acyl chlorides > Acid anhydrides > Esters > Amides > Carboxylate ions.

A more reactive derivative can be converted into a less reactive derivative, but the reverse reaction is generally not possible without special reagents.

Option (A): Ethyl acetate (an ester) reacts with ethylamine to form N-ethylacetamide (an amide). Since esters are more reactive than amides, this aminolysis reaction proceeds in good yield.

Option (B): Acetyl chloride reacts with acetate ion to form acetic anhydride. Since acyl chlorides are more reactive than acid anhydrides, this reaction proceeds readily.

Option (C): N-ethylacetamide (an amide) is reacted with ethanol to form acetic anhydride (or an ester). Amides are the least reactive among these derivatives and cannot be directly converted into an ester or an anhydride by simply reacting with an alcohol. Thus, this reaction will not proceed as indicated.

Option (D): Acetyl chloride reacts with ethanol to form ethyl acetate (an ester). Since acyl chlorides are more reactive than esters, this alcoholysis reaction proceeds easily.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is most reactive towards nucleophilic attack at the carbonyl carbon?
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a carbonyl compound towards nucleophilic attack depends on the magnitude of the positive charge (electrophilicity) on the carbonyl carbon. This is influenced by the inductive (-I) and resonance (+R) effects of the attached groups.

In acetyl chloride ($CH_3COCl$), the chlorine atom exerts a strong -I effect and a very weak +R effect (due to ineffective 2p-3p orbital overlap). This strongly withdraws electron density, making the carbonyl carbon highly electron-deficient and very susceptible to nucleophilic attack.

In methyl acetate ($CH_3COOCH_3$) and acetamide ($CH_3CONH_2$), the $-OCH_3$ and $-NH_2$ groups have significant +R effects that donate electron density to the carbonyl carbon, thereby reducing its electrophilicity. The +R effect is stronger in amides than in esters.

In acetone ($CH_3COCH_3$), the methyl group exerts an electron-donating +I effect, which also decreases the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon.

Thus, the order of reactivity towards nucleophilic attack is: Acid chloride > Ketone > Ester > Amide.

Therefore, acetyl chloride is the most reactive.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following two-step reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
In the first step, benzoic acid reacts with thionyl chloride ($SOCl_2$). This is a standard method for converting carboxylic acids into acid chlorides. The hydroxyl group ($-OH$) of the carboxylic acid is replaced by a chlorine atom ($-Cl$), yielding benzoyl chloride ($C_6H_5COCl$).

In the second step, benzoyl chloride undergoes a nucleophilic acyl substitution reaction with ethylamine ($CH_3CH_2NH_2$). The nitrogen atom of ethylamine acts as a nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of benzoyl chloride. The chloride ion acts as a leaving group. Pyridine is added as a base to neutralize the $HCl$ byproduct formed during the reaction, driving it to completion.

The final product is an amide, specifically N-ethylbenzamide ($C_6H_5CONHCH_2CH_3$).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When ethane-1,2-dioic acid is heated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, the products formed are:
options: $\mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{HCOOH}$
$\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{HCOOH}$
$\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{HCOOH}$
$\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
questions.solution:
Ethane-1,2-dioic acid is commonly known as oxalic acid, $\mathrm{HOOC-COOH}$.

Concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ acts as a strong dehydrating agent.

When oxalic acid is heated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, it undergoes dehydration to yield carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and water.

The chemical reaction is:

$\mathrm{HOOC-COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}, \Delta} \mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

Answer: $\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following best explains why the reaction of a Grignard reagent with a carboxylic acid does not yield a secondary alcohol?
options: Grignard reagents exclusively react with aldehydes, ketones, esters, and epoxides.
The carboxylic acid is too sterically hindered for the Grignard reagent to attack.
The Grignard reagent acts as a strong base, so an acid-base reaction occurs preferentially.
The carboxylic acid is not electrophilic enough to undergo nucleophilic addition.
questions.solution:
Grignard reagents ($RMgX$) are not only strong nucleophiles but also very strong bases. Carboxylic acids ($RCOOH$) contain a highly acidic proton. When a Grignard reagent is mixed with a carboxylic acid, an acid-base reaction occurs much faster than nucleophilic addition to the carbonyl carbon. The Grignard reagent deprotonates the carboxylic acid to form an alkane ($RH$) and a carboxylate salt ($RCOO^{-} \, ^{+}MgX$). The resulting carboxylate anion is resonance stabilized and lacks sufficient electrophilicity to undergo further nucleophilic attack by another equivalent of the Grignard reagent. Thus, the acid-base reaction prevents the formation of a secondary alcohol.

Answer: The Grignard reagent acts as a strong base, so an acid-base reaction occurs preferentially.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Heating sodium formate at $360^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ yields:
options: $\mathrm{CO}_2$
$\mathrm{CO}$
No reaction
Sodium oxalate
questions.solution:
When sodium formate is heated to about $360^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, it undergoes decomposition to form sodium oxalate and hydrogen gas.

The chemical equation for the reaction is:
$2 \mathrm{HCOONa} \xrightarrow{360^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{Na_2C_2O_4} + \mathrm{H_2} \uparrow$

Thus, the product obtained is sodium oxalate.

Answer: Sodium oxalate

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reaction sequences represents the best synthesis of the carboxylic acid shown below?
questions.solution:
The target molecule is 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid, $(CH_3)_3C-COOH$.

Evaluating the third sequence:

$CH_3COCH_3 + CH_3MgBr \rightarrow (CH_3)_3C-O^-MgBr^+$

$(CH_3)_3C-O^-MgBr^+ + H_3O^+ \rightarrow (CH_3)_3C-OH$

$(CH_3)_3C-OH + SOBr_2 \rightarrow (CH_3)_3C-Br$

$(CH_3)_3C-Br + Mg \rightarrow (CH_3)_3C-MgBr$

$(CH_3)_3C-MgBr + CO_2 \rightarrow (CH_3)_3C-COO^-MgBr^+$

$(CH_3)_3C-COO^-MgBr^+ + H_3O^+ \rightarrow (CH_3)_3C-COOH$

This sequence successfully produces the target molecule.

The first sequence is incorrect because using $KCN$ on the tertiary halide $(CH_3)_3C-Br$ leads predominantly to an elimination reaction, forming isobutylene instead of the required substitution product (nitrile).

The second and fourth sequences start with propanal ($CH_3CH_2CHO$), which will form derivatives with a sec-butyl group, ultimately yielding 2-methylbutanoic acid rather than the target molecule.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Heating methanoic acid with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ yields:
options: $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
$(\mathrm{COOH})_{2}$
CO
questions.solution:
Methanoic acid (formic acid, $\mathrm{HCOOH}$) when heated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ undergoes dehydration.

Concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ acts as a strong dehydrating agent and removes a molecule of water from methanoic acid to yield carbon monoxide ($\mathrm{CO}$).

The reaction is given by:
$\mathrm{HCOOH} \xrightarrow{\text{conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}, \Delta} \mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

Answer: CO

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is a bicyclic compound, specifically a fused system of two 5-membered rings: a tetrahydrofuran ring and a $\gamma$-lactone ring. Its structure is hexahydrofuro[2,3-b]furan-2-one.

Step 1: Reaction with aqueous NaOH
Aqueous NaOH hydrolyzes the lactone ring. The hydroxide ion attacks the carbonyl carbon, breaking the acyl-oxygen bond. This opens the $\gamma$-lactone ring, forming the sodium salt of a carboxylic acid and leaving a hydroxyl group at the bridgehead carbon (which is an acetal carbon).
The intermediate formed after acidification is (2-hydroxytetrahydrofuran-3-yl)acetic acid. This molecule contains a cyclic hemiacetal.

Step 2: Reaction with $H^+ / \Delta$
Under acidic conditions and heat, the cyclic hemiacetal opens up to its straight-chain aldehyde form.
The ring opening breaks the C-O bond of the tetrahydrofuran ring, converting the hemiacetal carbon into an aldehyde group (-CHO) and the ether oxygen into a primary alcohol (-OH).
The resulting open-chain intermediate is 3-formyl-5-hydroxypentanoic acid, with the structure: $\text{HO-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH(CHO)-CH}_2\text{-COOH}$.

Step 3: Intramolecular Lactonization
In the presence of acid and heat, the primary alcohol group (-OH at C5) reacts with the carboxylic acid group (-COOH at C1) to undergo intramolecular esterification (lactonization).
This forms a stable 6-membered $\delta$-lactone ring (tetrahydro-2H-pyran-2-one).
Tracing the positions: the -CHO group is attached to C3 of the pentanoic acid chain. In the newly formed 6-membered lactone ring, the carbonyl carbon is position 2, the adjacent CH$_2$ is position 3, and the CH bearing the formyl group becomes position 4.
Thus, the major product is 4-formyltetrahydro-2H-pyran-2-one.

Comparing this with the given options, Option (D) correctly shows a 6-membered lactone ring with an aldehyde (-CHO) group at the 4-position.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the final product (C) in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The starting material is 2,4,6-triphenylbenzoyl chloride.

Treatment with anhydrous $\text{AlCl}_3$ generates an electrophilic acylium ion at the carbonyl carbon. This acylium ion undergoes an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts acylation by attacking the ortho position of one of the adjacent phenyl rings.

This cyclization forms a five-membered ring, resulting in a fluorenone derivative as intermediate (A). The original central benzene ring becomes one of the outer rings of the fluorenone core. The fusion occurs at the original carbons C1 (attached to the carbonyl) and C2 (attached to the phenyl ring that was attacked). The remaining free carbons of this ring correspond to C6, C5, C4, and C3 of the original central ring, ordered from top to bottom. The unreacted phenyl groups are at the original positions C6 and C4. Therefore, in the fluorenone product, the phenyl groups are located at the first and third free carbons (positions 1 and 3 of the fluorenone system). Intermediate (A) is 1,3-diphenyl-9H-fluoren-9-one.

Reaction with hydrazine ($\text{H}_2\text{N-NH}_2$) forms a hydrazone intermediate (B).

Subsequent treatment with $\text{KOH}$ and heat in ethylene glycol constitutes a Wolff-Kishner reduction. This completely reduces the hydrazone group to a methylene ($-\text{CH}_2-$) group.

The final product (C) is 1,3-diphenyl-9H-fluorene. Option (3) correctly shows the fluorene core with phenyl substituents at the 1 and 3 positions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product (A) in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves two main steps:

1. The first step is the reaction of propanoic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$) with $\text{Br}_2$ and $\text{PBr}_3$. This is the Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) reaction, which selectively brominates the $\alpha$-carbon of the carboxylic acid. The product formed is $\alpha$-bromopropanoic acid.
$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Br}_2/\text{PBr}_3} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{Br})\text{COOH}$

2. The second step is the reaction of $\alpha$-bromopropanoic acid with alcoholic ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$). This is a nucleophilic substitution reaction ($S_N2$) where the ammonia molecule acts as a nucleophile and displaces the bromide ion from the $\alpha$-position. The carboxylic acid group remains unaffected under these mild conditions.
$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{Br})\text{COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{NH}_3\text{ (alc.)}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{NH}_2)\text{COOH}$

The final major product (A) is $\alpha$-aminopropanoic acid (alanine).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of the $^{18}\text{O}$-labeled ester shown below:


Identify the major products A (an acid) and B.
options: and
and
and
and
questions.solution:
The given ester is formed from acetic acid and a tertiary alcohol (2-phenylpropan-2-ol).

In the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of esters, the mechanism depends on the nature of the alkyl group. For esters with primary or secondary alkyl groups, the reaction typically proceeds via the $A_{AC}2$ mechanism (acyl-oxygen cleavage). However, when the alkyl group can form a highly stable carbocation (such as a tertiary, allylic, or benzylic carbocation), the reaction proceeds via the $A_{AL}1$ mechanism (alkyl-oxygen cleavage).

In this case, the alkyl group is the cumyl group ($-\text{C}(\text{Ph})(\text{CH}_3)_2$), which forms a highly stable tertiary carbocation due to resonance stabilization from the phenyl ring and hyperconjugation from the methyl groups. Therefore, the hydrolysis will follow the $A_{AL}1$ mechanism.

The steps are as follows:
1. Protonation of the ester oxygen.
2. Cleavage of the alkyl-oxygen bond to form the stable cumyl carbocation $\text{Ph}-\text{C}^{+}(\text{CH}_3)_2$ and the labeled acetic acid molecule $\text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-^{18}\text{OH}$. Since the alkyl-oxygen bond breaks, the $^{18}\text{O}$ atom remains attached to the acyl group.
3. The carbocation then reacts with a molecule of water (which is unlabeled) from the solvent to form the unlabeled tertiary alcohol $\text{Ph}-\text{C}(\text{OH})(\text{CH}_3)_2$.

Thus, the major products are $\text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-^{18}\text{OH}$ (an acid) and $\text{Ph}-\text{C}(\text{OH})(\text{CH}_3)_2$ (an alcohol). This corresponds to the products shown in Option (A).

Answer: and

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds has the highest boiling point?
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{H}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$
questions.solution:
The boiling point of a compound depends on the strength of its intermolecular forces.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (Propane) is non-polar and has only weak London dispersion forces, resulting in the lowest boiling point.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}$ (Acetone) is polar and has dipole-dipole interactions, giving it a higher boiling point than propane but lower than compounds with hydrogen bonding.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ (Ethanol) and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{H}$ (Acetic acid) both exhibit intermolecular hydrogen bonding.

Acetic acid has stronger hydrogen bonding than ethanol because the electron-withdrawing carbonyl group makes the O-H bond more polar. Additionally, acetic acid molecules form stable hydrogen-bonded dimers in the liquid phase, which significantly increases the effective molecular mass and the strength of intermolecular forces.

Therefore, $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{H}$ has the highest boiling point among the given compounds.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The reaction sequence proceeds as follows:

1. Grignard Reaction: The starting material is methyl cyclohexanecarboxylate, an ester. When treated with excess phenylmagnesium bromide ($\text{PhMgBr}$), the Grignard reagent attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon. The first equivalent of $\text{PhMgBr}$ substitutes the methoxy group ($\text{-OMe}$) to form a ketone, cyclohexyl phenyl ketone. A second equivalent of $\text{PhMgBr}$ then attacks the ketone to form a tertiary alkoxide. Upon hydrolysis ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$), the alkoxide is protonated to yield the tertiary alcohol, cyclohexyl(diphenyl)methanol.

2. Dehydration: The tertiary alcohol is then treated with acid ($\text{H}^{\oplus}$) and heat. The hydroxyl group is protonated and leaves as a water molecule, generating a carbocation at the exocyclic carbon. This carbocation, $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11}\text{-C}^{\oplus}(\text{Ph})_2$, is highly stable due to resonance delocalization from the two adjacent phenyl rings.

3. Elimination: To form the alkene, a proton is eliminated from the adjacent carbon. The only adjacent carbon with a proton is the C1 carbon of the cyclohexane ring. Loss of this proton results in the formation of a double bond between the cyclohexane ring and the exocyclic carbon.

The final product is cyclohexylidene(diphenyl)methane, which features an exocyclic double bond that is highly stabilized by conjugation with the two phenyl rings. This corresponds to the structure in Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following groups in decreasing order of their leaving group ability:

(I)

(II)

(III) $-\mathrm{OMe}$

(IV)
options: $\text{(I)} > \text{(II)} > \text{(IV)} > \text{(III)}$
$\text{(IV)} > \text{(I)} > \text{(II)} > \text{(III)}$
$\text{(IV)} > \text{(I)} > \text{(III)} > \text{(II)}$
$\text{(II)} > \text{(I)} > \text{(IV)} > \text{(III)}$
questions.solution:
The leaving group ability of an anion is inversely proportional to its basicity. A weaker base is a more stable anion and thus a better leaving group.

The basicity of an anion can be compared by looking at the acidic strength of its conjugate acid. A stronger acid will have a weaker, more stable conjugate base.

The conjugate acids for the given leaving groups are:
(I) $p$-Toluenesulfonic acid ($p$-$\text{CH}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{SO}_3\text{H}$)
(II) Acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$)
(III) Methanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$)
(IV) Trifluoromethanesulfonic acid ($\text{CF}_3\text{SO}_3\text{H}$)

Comparing their acidic strengths:
$\text{CF}_3\text{SO}_3\text{H}$ is a superacid. Its conjugate base (triflate) is highly stabilized by the strong $-I$ effect of the $-\text{CF}_3$ group and resonance over three oxygen atoms.
$p$-$\text{CH}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{SO}_3\text{H}$ is also a strong acid, but weaker than triflic acid because the $p$-tolyl group is electron-donating ($+I$ and hyperconjugation) compared to the $-\text{CF}_3$ group.
$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ is a weak acid, but stronger than methanol due to the resonance stabilization of the acetate anion over two oxygen atoms.
$\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ is the weakest acid among them, as the methoxide ion lacks resonance stabilization and is destabilized by the $+I$ effect of the methyl group.

The order of acidic strength is:
$\text{CF}_3\text{SO}_3\text{H} > p\text{-CH}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{SO}_3\text{H} > \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} > \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$

Consequently, the order of basicity of their conjugate bases is:
$\text{CF}_3\text{SO}_3^- < p\text{-CH}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{SO}_3^- < \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- < \text{CH}_3\text{O}^-$

Therefore, the decreasing order of leaving group ability is:
$\text{CF}_3\text{SO}_3^- > p\text{-CH}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{SO}_3^- > \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- > \text{CH}_3\text{O}^-$

This corresponds to $\text{(IV)} > \text{(I)} > \text{(II)} > \text{(III)}$.

Answer: $\text{(IV)} > \text{(I)} > \text{(II)} > \text{(III)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 5-hydroxyheptanoic acid, which contains both a carboxylic acid group ($-COOH$) and a hydroxyl group ($-OH$) in the same molecule.

In the presence of an acid catalyst like HCl, such molecules undergo intramolecular esterification (lactonization). The lone pair on the oxygen atom of the hydroxyl group attacks the protonated carbonyl carbon of the carboxylic acid group, followed by the loss of a water molecule.

Since the hydroxyl group is located on the 5th carbon atom (counting the carboxyl carbon as C1), the intramolecular nucleophilic attack results in the formation of a stable 6-membered cyclic ester, known as a $\delta$-lactone.

The 5th carbon atom of the main chain is also attached to an ethyl group ($-CH_2CH_3$). In the resulting lactone ring, this ethyl group will appear as a substituent on the carbon atom adjacent to the ring oxygen.

Option (B) correctly represents this 6-membered lactone with an ethyl group at the correct position. Other options like substitution of $-OH$ by $-Cl$ (Option A) are not the major product because intramolecular ring formation to a stable 5- or 6-membered ring is kinetically and thermodynamically highly favored.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards nucleophilic acyl substitution:

(I)
(II)
(III)
(IV)
options: III > II > I > IV
III > IV > II > I
II > IV > I > III
II > III > IV > I
questions.solution:
The reactivity of acyl derivatives towards nucleophilic substitution depends on the leaving group ability of the group attached to the carbonyl carbon.

The leaving groups in the given compounds are:
(I) $-NH_2$
(II) $-Cl$
(III) $-OAc$ ($-O-CO-CH_3$)
(IV) $-OCH_3$

A weaker base is a better leaving group. The basic strength of the leaving groups follows the order:
$Cl^- < CH_3COO^- < CH_3O^- < NH_2^-$

Therefore, the leaving group ability follows the reverse order:
$Cl^- > CH_3COO^- > CH_3O^- > NH_2^-$

Hence, the order of reactivity towards nucleophilic acyl substitution is:
Acid chloride (II) > Acid anhydride (III) > Ester (IV) > Amide (I)

The correct order is II > III > IV > I.

Answer: II > III > IV > I

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction sequence:


Identify the major final product (Y).
questions.solution:
Reaction with $AlCl_3$ generates an electrophilic acylium ion from the acid chloride.

The benzene ring undergoes an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts acylation. The alkyl chain is ortho/para-directing and activating, while the formyl ($-CHO$) group is meta-directing and deactivating. Since they are para to each other, their directing effects reinforce each other, directing the acylium ion to the position ortho to the alkyl chain.

Cyclization at this position forms a fused six-membered ring, resulting in intermediate (X). The original alkyl attachment carbon becomes fusion carbon 4a, and the new ketone carbonyl is at position 1. The $-CHO$ group, originally para to the alkyl chain, occupies position 7 in the newly formed tetralone system.

Intermediate (X) is 7-formyl-1-tetralone.

The subsequent reaction with zinc amalgam ($Zn-Hg$) and concentrated $HCl$ is a Clemmensen reduction.

Clemmensen reduction completely reduces both the ketone and aldehyde carbonyl groups to alkane groups. The ring ketone is reduced to a methylene ($-CH_2-$) group, and the aromatic aldehyde is reduced to a methyl ($-CH_3$) group.

This complete reduction yields 7-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydronaphthalene (7-methyltetralin) as the major final product (Y). This corresponds to the structure in option (4), where the methyl substituent is at the 7-position.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
Reaction of acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{CO}_2\text{H}$) with $\text{SOCl}_2$ produces acetyl chloride ($\text{CH}_3\text{COCl}$).

Acetyl chloride reacts with the primary amine group of 3-methylaniline to undergo N-acylation, forming the amide N-(3-methylphenyl)acetamide.

Reduction of this amide with $\text{LiAlH}_4$ converts the carbonyl group to a methylene group, changing the $-\text{NHCOCH}_3$ group to an $-\text{NHCH}_2\text{CH}_3$ group.

The final product is N-ethyl-3-methylaniline, which corresponds to the structure in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When benzene is treated with acetic anhydride in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, the major product formed is:
options: Benzoic acid
Acetophenone
Benzophenone
Ethyl phenyl ketone
questions.solution:
The reaction of benzene with acetic anhydride in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride is an example of Friedel-Crafts acylation.

The electrophile generated is the acetyl cation ($CH_3C^+O$).

Benzene undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution with the acetyl cation to form acetophenone.

The chemical equation is:
$C_6H_6 + (CH_3CO)_2O \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5COCH_3 + CH_3COOH$

The major product formed is acetophenone.

Answer: Acetophenone

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is phthalic acid (benzene-1,2-dicarboxylic acid).

The reagent $P_2O_5$ (phosphorus pentoxide) acts as a strong dehydrating agent.

When phthalic acid is heated with $P_2O_5$, it undergoes intramolecular dehydration. A molecule of water ($H_2O$) is eliminated from the two adjacent carboxylic acid ($-COOH$) groups.

This elimination leads to the formation of a five-membered cyclic anhydride ring fused to the benzene ring. The resulting major product is phthalic anhydride.

The structure of phthalic anhydride corresponds to the molecule shown in option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:

(i) $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{H}^{+}$
questions.solution:
The given reactant is benzoic acid ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$).

In the first step, benzoic acid reacts with thionyl chloride ($\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$) to undergo a nucleophilic acyl substitution, replacing the hydroxyl group with a chloride ion to form benzoyl chloride.
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{SOCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COCl} + \mathrm{SO}_{2} + \mathrm{HCl}$

In the second step, benzoyl chloride reacts with ethanol ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$) in an acidic medium. This is an alcoholysis reaction where the acid chloride is converted into an ester.
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COCl} + \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOCH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3} + \mathrm{HCl}$

The final major product is ethyl benzoate, which corresponds to the structure in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the given reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts acylation.

Step 1: The acid chloride reacts with the Lewis acid catalyst $\text{AlCl}_3$ to generate an electrophilic acylium ion.
$\text{Ph}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{COCl} + \text{AlCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ph}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}^{+}=\text{O} + \text{AlCl}_4^{-}$

Step 2: The acylium ion acts as an electrophile and attacks the ortho position of the benzene ring. This intramolecular attack forms a new carbon-carbon bond, creating a 5-membered ring fused to the benzene ring.

Step 3: Loss of a proton from the intermediate sigma complex restores aromaticity, yielding the ketone complexed with aluminum.

Step 4: Addition of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ hydrolyzes the aluminum complex to give the final product, 1-indanone ($\alpha$-indanone).

The structure of 1-indanone matches option (B), where the carbonyl group is directly attached to the aromatic ring within the newly formed 5-membered ring.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following structures represents benzoic acid?
questions.solution:
Benzoic acid consists of a benzene ring attached to a carboxyl group ($-COOH$).

The carboxyl group can also be represented as $-CO_2H$.

Looking at the given options:
(A) represents phenyl formate ($-OCHO$).
(B) represents benzoic acid ($-CO_2H$).
(C) and (D) represent other arrangements, such as phenyl formate ($-OCOH$).

Therefore, the structure with the $-CO_2H$ group correctly represents benzoic acid.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is isopropylbenzene (cumene).

The reagent $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ in the presence of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ and heat is a strong oxidizing agent.

When alkylbenzenes are treated with strong oxidizing agents, the entire alkyl side chain is oxidized to a carboxyl group ($-\text{COOH}$), provided there is at least one benzylic hydrogen atom present.

In isopropylbenzene, the benzylic carbon is bonded to one hydrogen atom. Therefore, the isopropyl group is completely oxidized to form a carboxylic acid group.

The major product formed is benzoic acid.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following species does not represent an intermediate in the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of propionamide to propanoic acid?
questions.solution:
The acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of propionamide to propanoic acid proceeds via the following steps:

1. Protonation of the carbonyl oxygen of propionamide forms a resonance-stabilized cation:
$CH_3CH_2CONH_2 + H^+ \rightleftharpoons CH_3CH_2C(=O^+H)NH_2 \leftrightarrow CH_3CH_2C(OH)=N^+H_2$
This matches the species in Option (A).

2. Nucleophilic attack by water on the electrophilic carbon, followed by proton transfer, yields a tetrahedral intermediate where the nitrogen is protonated to make it a good leaving group:
$CH_3CH_2C(OH)(OH_2^+)NH_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_3CH_2C(OH)_2N^+H_3$
This matches the species in Option (D).

3. Expulsion of the leaving group ($NH_3$) forms protonated propanoic acid:
$CH_3CH_2C(OH)_2N^+H_3 \rightleftharpoons CH_3CH_2C(=O^+H)OH + NH_3$
This matches the species in Option (B).

Option (C) represents an anionic tetrahedral intermediate ($CH_3CH_2C(O^-)(NH_2)(OH)$). In an acidic medium, intermediates are either neutral or positively charged. Anionic intermediates are characteristic of base-catalyzed hydrolysis, not acid-catalyzed hydrolysis.

Therefore, the species in Option (C) does not represent an intermediate in this acid-catalyzed reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the given reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Friedel-Crafts acylation of anisole (methoxybenzene) with succinic anhydride.

Step 1: Succinic anhydride reacts with the Lewis acid catalyst $\text{AlCl}_3$ to undergo ring opening, generating an electrophilic acylium ion intermediate: $^{+}\text{C}(=\text{O})\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOAlCl}_3^{-}$.

Step 2: The $-\text{OCH}_3$ group on anisole is a strong electron-donating group via resonance ($+R$ effect), which activates the benzene ring towards electrophilic aromatic substitution and directs the incoming electrophile to the ortho and para positions.

Step 3: Due to steric hindrance caused by the bulky $-\text{OCH}_3$ group and the large size of the electrophile, the attack occurs predominantly at the less hindered para position.

Step 4: Subsequent hydrolysis (addition of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$) removes the aluminum complex, yielding the final keto-acid product, 4-(4-methoxyphenyl)-4-oxobutanoic acid.

The structure of the major product is $p\text{-CH}_3\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$, which corresponds to option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in order of increasing boiling point:
(I)
(II)
(III)
options: (I) < (III) < (II)
(III) < (I) < (II)
(II) < (III) < (I)
(II) < (I) < (III)
questions.solution:
The given compounds are:
(I) Propanamide
(II) Propanoyl chloride
(III) Propanoic acid

The boiling point of a compound depends on the strength of its intermolecular forces.

Propanoyl chloride (II) is a polar molecule but lacks the ability to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds. It only has dipole-dipole interactions, resulting in the lowest boiling point.

Propanoic acid (III) can form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds, often existing as dimers, which significantly raises its boiling point compared to the acid chloride.

Propanamide (I) has two N-H bonds and a C=O group, allowing it to form a more extensive network of intermolecular hydrogen bonds than propanoic acid. Consequently, it has the highest boiling point among the three.

Therefore, the order of increasing boiling point is (II) < (III) < (I).

Answer: (II) < (III) < (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction of benzene with an acid chloride in the presence of anhydrous $AlCl_3$ is a Friedel-Crafts acylation.

The Lewis acid $AlCl_3$ facilitates the generation of an electrophilic acylium ion from butyryl chloride:

$CH_3CH_2CH_2COCl + AlCl_3 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2C^+=O + AlCl_4^-$

The acylium ion is resonance stabilized and does not undergo rearrangement, unlike alkyl carbocations.

Electrophilic aromatic substitution of the acylium ion on the benzene ring yields 1-phenylbutan-1-one as the major product.

This corresponds to the structure given in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When ethane-1,2-dioic acid is heated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, the products formed are:
options: $\mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{HCOOH}$
$\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{HCOOH}$
$\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{HCOOH}$
questions.solution:
Ethane-1,2-dioic acid is commonly known as oxalic acid, with the chemical formula $\mathrm{HOOC-COOH}$.

Concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ acts as a strong dehydrating agent. When oxalic acid is heated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, it undergoes dehydration to yield carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and water.

The chemical reaction is given by:

$\mathrm{HOOC-COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}, \Delta} \mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

Thus, the products formed are $\mathrm{CO}$, $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, and $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When sodium formate is heated with soda lime, the product obtained is
options: no reaction
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
sodium oxalate
$\mathrm{H}_{2}$
questions.solution:
Sodium formate is $\mathrm{HCOONa}$.

Soda lime is a mixture of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ and $\mathrm{CaO}$.

When sodium formate is heated with soda lime, it undergoes decarboxylation to produce hydrogen gas and sodium carbonate.

The reaction is:
$\mathrm{HCOONa} + \mathrm{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CaO}, \Delta} \mathrm{Na_2CO_3} + \mathrm{H_2} \uparrow$

Thus, the product obtained is $\mathrm{H_2}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{H}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Heating methanoic acid with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ produces:
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$
$(\mathrm{COOH})_{2}$
CO
$\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
questions.solution:
Methanoic acid (formic acid, $\mathrm{HCOOH}$) when heated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ undergoes dehydration. Concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ acts as a strong dehydrating agent and removes a molecule of water from methanoic acid.

The chemical reaction is given by:
$\mathrm{HCOOH} \xrightarrow{\text{conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}, \Delta} \mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

Thus, carbon monoxide ($\mathrm{CO}$) gas is produced.

Answer: CO

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following esters will undergo alkaline hydrolysis most rapidly?
questions.solution:
Alkaline hydrolysis of an ester involves the nucleophilic attack of a hydroxide ion ($OH^-$) on the carbonyl carbon. The rate of this reaction is directly proportional to the magnitude of the positive charge (electrophilicity) on the carbonyl carbon.

Electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) increase the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, thereby facilitating the nucleophilic attack and increasing the rate of hydrolysis. Conversely, electron-donating groups (EDG) decrease the positive charge, slowing down the reaction.

Let us analyze the substituents present at the para position in the given esters:
1. In option (B), the $-NO_2$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group due to both its $-I$ (inductive) and $-R$ (resonance) effects.
2. In option (A), the $-Cl$ group is a weak electron-withdrawing group ($-I > +R$).
3. In option (D), there is no substituent ($-H$), which serves as the reference.
4. In option (C), the $-CH_3$ group is an electron-donating group due to its $+I$ effect and hyperconjugation.

The order of electron-withdrawing strength is $-NO_2 > -Cl > -H > -CH_3$.

Therefore, the carbonyl carbon in methyl p-nitrobenzoate (B) is the most electrophilic, making it the most reactive towards alkaline hydrolysis.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is the most acidic (i.e., has the lowest $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ value)?
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of substituted benzoic acids depends on the stability of their conjugate base (carboxylate anion).

Electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) stabilize the carboxylate anion by dispersing the negative charge, thereby increasing the acidity. Conversely, electron-donating groups (EDG) destabilize the anion by intensifying the negative charge, which decreases the acidity.

Let us analyze the substituents present at the para position in the given options:
(A) Benzoic acid: No substituent.
(B) p-Nitrobenzoic acid: The $-\text{NO}_2$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group. It exerts both $-R$ (resonance) and $-I$ (inductive) effects, strongly stabilizing the conjugate base.
(C) p-Toluic acid: The $-\text{CH}_3$ group is an electron-donating group. It exerts $+I$ and hyperconjugation effects, destabilizing the conjugate base.
(D) p-Aminobenzoic acid: The $-\text{NH}_2$ group is a strong electron-donating group due to its $+R$ effect (lone pair donation), which strongly destabilizes the conjugate base.

Therefore, the $-\text{NO}_2$ group in p-nitrobenzoic acid makes it the most acidic among the given compounds, corresponding to the lowest $\text{pK}_\text{a}$ value.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) reaction involves the use of phosphorus and a halogen. For example:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{COOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Cl}_{2}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHClCOOH}$
The primary function of phosphorus in this reaction is:
options: none of these
to form $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$, which directly carries out halogenation at the $\alpha$-carbon atom
to form $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$, which converts the $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group into a $-\mathrm{COCl}$ group
to act as a catalyst
questions.solution:
In the Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) reaction, the $\alpha$-halogenation of carboxylic acids takes place.

In the first step of the reaction, red phosphorus reacts with the halogen (e.g., $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$) to form phosphorus trihalide ($\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$).

The $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ then reacts with the carboxylic acid to convert the $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group into an acid chloride ($-\mathrm{COCl}$) group.

Acid chlorides undergo enolization much more readily than carboxylic acids. The enol form then reacts with the halogen to undergo $\alpha$-halogenation, forming an $\alpha$-halo acid chloride, which subsequently exchanges with the unreacted carboxylic acid to yield the $\alpha$-halo carboxylic acid.

Therefore, the primary function of phosphorus is to form $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$, which converts the $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group into a $-\mathrm{COCl}$ group.

Answer: to form $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$, which converts the $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group into a $-\mathrm{COCl}$ group

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of esters is a reversible reaction. Which of the following statements is true regarding the base-promoted hydrolysis of esters?
options: It is irreversible because the carboxylate ion is resonance stabilized.
It is a reversible reaction.
It is irreversible because salts of carboxylic acids have high melting points.
It is irreversible because salts of carboxylic acids are insoluble.
questions.solution:
Base-promoted hydrolysis of esters, also known as saponification, involves the nucleophilic attack of a hydroxide ion on the carbonyl carbon of the ester, leading to the formation of a carboxylic acid and an alkoxide ion.

The alkoxide ion, being a strong base, rapidly abstracts a proton from the carboxylic acid to form an alcohol and a carboxylate ion.

The resulting carboxylate ion is highly resonance stabilized, distributing the negative charge equally over the two oxygen atoms. This resonance stabilization makes the carbonyl carbon significantly less electrophilic, preventing the alcohol from attacking it to reform the ester.

Consequently, the formation of the resonance-stabilized carboxylate ion drives the reaction to completion, making the base-promoted hydrolysis of esters an irreversible process.

Answer: It is irreversible because the carboxylate ion is resonance stabilized.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following reactions are feasible?
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{HCl}$
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOCH}_{3} + \mathrm{HBr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COBr} + \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CONH}_{2} + \mathrm{HBr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COBr} + \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(IV) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOCOCH}_{3} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$
options: (I), (III) and (IV)
(I) and (IV)
(I), (II), (III) and (IV)
(I), (II) and (IV)
questions.solution:
The feasibility of nucleophilic acyl substitution depends on the leaving group ability. A weaker base is a better leaving group.

The order of basic strength of the leaving groups is $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} < \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{-} < \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{-} < \mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$.

Therefore, the leaving group ability and the reactivity of acid derivatives follow the order:
Acid chloride $>$ Acid anhydride $>$ Ester $>$ Amide.

A more reactive acid derivative can be converted into a less reactive one, but the reverse is not feasible.

(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{HCl}$ : Acid chloride is converted to carboxylic acid. This is highly feasible.

(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOCH}_{3} + \mathrm{HBr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COBr} + \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$ : Ester is converted to acid bromide. This is not feasible because acid bromides are more reactive than esters.

(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CONH}_{2} + \mathrm{HBr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COBr} + \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ : Amide is converted to acid bromide. This is not feasible because acid bromides are much more reactive than amides.

(IV) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOCOCH}_{3} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$ : Acid anhydride is converted to carboxylic acid. This is feasible.

Hence, only reactions (I) and (IV) are feasible.

Answer: (I) and (IV)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds will produce effervescence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ when treated with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$?
options: Only 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
Neither HCOOH nor 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
Only HCOOH
Both HCOOH and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
questions.solution:
Compounds that are stronger acids than carbonic acid ($\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$) will react with aqueous $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ to produce effervescence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas.

The $pK_a$ of carbonic acid is approximately $6.4$.

Formic acid ($\mathrm{HCOOH}$) has a $pK_a$ of $3.75$, making it a stronger acid than carbonic acid. It reacts with $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ to release $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.

2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid) contains three strongly electron-withdrawing $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ groups on the benzene ring, which highly stabilize the phenoxide ion. Its $pK_a$ is $0.38$, making it a much stronger acid than carbonic acid. It also reacts with $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ to release $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.

Therefore, both $\mathrm{HCOOH}$ and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol will produce effervescence of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ when treated with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$.

Answer: Both HCOOH and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the most suitable starting materials and conditions for the preparation of the following compound:
options: Any of the above
questions.solution:
The target compound is 4-nitrobenzophenone.

In reaction (1), nitrobenzene is used as the substrate. Nitrobenzene is highly deactivated due to the strongly electron-withdrawing $-NO_2$ group and does not undergo Friedel-Crafts acylation.

In reaction (2), nitration of benzophenone takes place. The carbonyl group is a meta-directing and deactivating group. Thus, the incoming electrophile will attach at the meta position, yielding 3-nitrobenzophenone as the major product, rather than the desired para isomer.

In reaction (4), benzene undergoes Friedel-Crafts acylation with 4-nitrobenzoyl chloride in the presence of anhydrous $AlCl_3$. Benzene is sufficiently reactive to undergo this reaction, successfully yielding the target product, 4-nitrobenzophenone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents is most preferred for converting a carboxylic acid into an acid chloride?
options: $\mathrm{HCl}$
$\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$
$\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$
$\mathrm{ClCOCOCl}$
questions.solution:
The conversion of a carboxylic acid into an acid chloride using thionyl chloride is represented by the equation

$RCOOH + SOCl_2 \rightarrow RCOCl + SO_2 \uparrow + HCl \uparrow$

Thionyl chloride ($SOCl_2$) is the most preferred reagent for this reaction.

The byproducts formed, sulphur dioxide ($SO_2$) and hydrogen chloride ($HCl$), are gases that readily escape from the reaction mixture.

This leaves behind the acid chloride in a nearly pure state, eliminating the need for complex purification steps.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements is true regarding the amount of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ required during Friedel-Crafts acetylation using acetyl chloride compared to acetic anhydride?
options: Acetylation with acetyl chloride requires a greater amount.
The relative amount required cannot be definitely stated.
Both require the same amount.
Acetylation with acetic anhydride requires a greater amount.
questions.solution:
In Friedel-Crafts acetylation using acetyl chloride, slightly more than one equivalent of anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ is required because the product ketone forms a complex with $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$.

When acetic anhydride is used as the acetylating agent, the reaction produces acetic acid as a byproduct. Both the product ketone and the byproduct acetic acid form stable complexes with $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$.

Therefore, slightly more than two equivalents of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ are required when acetic anhydride is used.

Thus, acetylation with acetic anhydride requires a greater amount of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$.

Answer: Acetylation with acetic anhydride requires a greater amount.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Heating sodium formate at $360^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ yields
options: $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
no action
$\mathrm{CO}$
sodium oxalate
questions.solution:
When sodium formate is heated at $360^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, it undergoes a coupling reaction with the elimination of hydrogen gas to form sodium oxalate.

$2 \mathrm{HCOONa} \xrightarrow{360^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{Na_2C_2O_4} + \mathrm{H_2} \uparrow$

Thus, the product obtained is sodium oxalate.

Answer: sodium oxalate

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product $\mathrm{A}$ in the following reaction:
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$
questions.solution:
The given reactant is diethyl carbonate, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{O}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5$.

When diethyl carbonate reacts with Grignard reagents, it undergoes successive nucleophilic acyl substitution reactions.

Step 1: The first equivalent of methylmagnesium bromide ($\text{CH}_3\text{MgBr}$) attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of diethyl carbonate. The tetrahedral intermediate collapses by expelling an ethoxide ion ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{O}^-$), resulting in the formation of an ester (ethyl acetate).
$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{O}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{CH}_3\text{MgBr} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OMgBr}$

Step 2: The second equivalent of $\text{CH}_3\text{MgBr}$ attacks the carbonyl carbon of the newly formed ethyl acetate. The intermediate again collapses by expelling the second ethoxide ion, yielding a ketone (acetone).
$\text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{CH}_3\text{MgBr} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{CH}_3 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OMgBr}$

Since exactly two equivalents of the Grignard reagent are provided, the reaction stops at the ketone stage based on the given stoichiometry. A third equivalent would be required to convert the ketone into a tertiary alcohol (tert-butyl alcohol).

Thus, the final product $\text{A}$ is acetone, $\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3$.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOCH}_{3} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ (reaction involves acyl-oxygen fission)

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOCH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{OH}^{-}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{-} + \mathrm{HOCH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}$ (configuration of the reactant is retained)
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOC}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{OH}^{-}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{-} + \mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$ (reaction involves acyl-oxygen fission)
questions.solution:
In the reaction shown in option (A), the ester undergoes hydrolysis in the presence of a base ($\mathrm{OH}^{-}$). The base is consumed stoichiometrically to form the carboxylate ion ($\mathrm{R-COO}^{-}$) and is not regenerated at the end of the reaction. Therefore, it is a base-promoted reaction (saponification), not a base-catalyzed reaction. Thus, statement (A) is incorrect.

In option (B), Fischer esterification proceeds via the $A_{AC}2$ mechanism, which involves the cleavage of the acyl-oxygen bond of the carboxylic acid. This statement is correct.

In options (C) and (D), the basic hydrolysis of esters typically proceeds via the $B_{AC}2$ mechanism, which involves acyl-oxygen cleavage. Because the alkyl-oxygen bond remains intact, the stereocenter in the alkyl group is unaffected, and its configuration is retained. These statements are correct.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following carboxylic acids in decreasing order of their acidic character:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of carboxylic acids depends on the stability of their conjugate base (carboxylate anion).

1. (v) 2,6-Dihydroxybenzoic acid: The conjugate base is highly stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding from two ortho hydroxyl (-OH) groups. Additionally, the strong steric hindrance (ortho effect) forces the carboxylate group out of the plane of the benzene ring, preventing destabilizing resonance from the ring. This makes it the most acidic.

2. (ii) o-Hydroxybenzoic acid (Salicylic acid): The conjugate base is stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding from one ortho -OH group, along with the ortho effect. It is highly acidic, but less so than (v).

3. (iv) o-Methoxybenzoic acid: The ortho -OCH$_3$ group exerts an ortho effect (steric inhibition of resonance), which forces the -COOH group out of the plane, reducing the electron-donating resonance from the ring. Combined with the -I effect of the oxygen atom, it is more acidic than benzoic acid. However, it lacks the intramolecular hydrogen bonding seen in (ii) and (v).

4. (i) Benzoic acid: This serves as the reference compound.

5. (iii) p-Hydroxybenzoic acid: The -OH group is at the para position, where its strong +R (resonance) effect dominates over its -I effect. This increases the electron density on the carboxylate group, destabilizing the conjugate base and making it less acidic than benzoic acid.

Therefore, the decreasing order of acidic character is (v) > (ii) > (iv) > (i) > (iii).

Answer: (v) > (ii) > (iv) > (i) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Choose the correct statement regarding the acidic character of acetic acid ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$) and peroxyacetic acid ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOOH}$).
options: Peroxyacetic acid is a weaker acid than acetic acid because its conjugate base is less stable than the acetate ion.
Peroxyacetic acid is a stronger acid than acetic acid because it contains an additional electronegative oxygen atom.
Peroxyacetic acid is a stronger acid than acetic acid because its conjugate base is a weaker base than the acetate ion.
Both acids are equally strong.
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of an acid depends on the stability of its conjugate base.

When acetic acid ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$) loses a proton, it forms the acetate ion ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{-}$). The negative charge on the acetate ion is highly stabilized by resonance, as it is delocalized equally over two electronegative oxygen atoms.

When peroxyacetic acid ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOOH}$) loses a proton, it forms the peroxyacetate ion ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOO}^{-}$). In this ion, the negative charge is localized on the terminal oxygen atom. It cannot be delocalized via resonance with the carbonyl group because of the intervening oxygen atom.

Since the conjugate base of peroxyacetic acid is less stable than the resonance-stabilized acetate ion, peroxyacetic acid is a weaker acid than acetic acid.

Answer: Peroxyacetic acid is a weaker acid than acetic acid because its conjugate base is less stable than the acetate ion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: To obtain the maximum yield of the acid amide in the reaction $\mathrm{RCOCl} + \mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{RCONH}_{2}$, which of the following conditions is most appropriate?
options: The acid chloride and ammonia are reacted in a 2:1 molar ratio.
The acid chloride and ammonia are reacted in an equimolar ratio.
All three of the above ratios give a nearly similar yield.
The acid chloride and ammonia are reacted in a 1:2 molar ratio.
questions.solution:
The reaction between an acid chloride and ammonia proceeds as follows:

$\mathrm{RCOCl} + \mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{RCONH}_{2} + \mathrm{HCl}$

The $\mathrm{HCl}$ produced during the reaction is an acid and will immediately react with the unreacted basic ammonia to form ammonium chloride:

$\mathrm{NH}_{3} + \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4}\mathrm{Cl}$

Adding the two equations gives the overall reaction:

$\mathrm{RCOCl} + 2\mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{RCONH}_{2} + \mathrm{NH}_{4}\mathrm{Cl}$

For every mole of acid chloride, two moles of ammonia are consumed (one acts as a nucleophile to form the amide, and the other acts as a base to neutralize the $\mathrm{HCl}$ byproduct). Therefore, to obtain the maximum yield of the acid amide, the acid chloride and ammonia must be reacted in a $1:2$ molar ratio.

Answer: The acid chloride and ammonia are reacted in a 1:2 molar ratio.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following represents the correct order of relative stability of the given acid derivatives towards nucleophilic attack?
options: Acid chloride > Acid anhydride > Ester > Amide
Acid chloride > Ester > Acid anhydride > Amide
Amide > Acid anhydride > Ester > Acid chloride
Amide > Ester > Acid anhydride > Acid chloride
questions.solution:
The reactivity of acid derivatives towards nucleophilic acyl substitution depends on the leaving group ability and the resonance stabilization of the acyl group.

The leaving group ability follows the order: $\text{Cl}^- > \text{RCOO}^- > \text{RO}^- > \text{NH}_2^-$.

The resonance stabilization of the carbonyl group by the attached heteroatom follows the order: $-\text{NH}_2 > -\text{OR} > -\text{OCOR} > -\text{Cl}$. Greater resonance stabilization decreases the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, leading to lower reactivity and higher stability.

Thus, the order of reactivity towards nucleophilic attack is: Acid chloride $>$ Acid anhydride $>$ Ester $>$ Amide.

Since stability is inversely proportional to reactivity, the correct order of relative stability is: Amide $>$ Ester $>$ Acid anhydride $>$ Acid chloride.

Answer: Amide > Ester > Acid anhydride > Acid chloride

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements are true regarding the hydrolysis of acetic anhydride?

(I) It is more easily hydrolyzed in an acidic medium than in a neutral medium.
(II) It is more easily hydrolyzed in an alkaline medium than in a neutral medium.
(III) It is hydrolyzed equally easily in all three media.
(IV) It is more easily hydrolyzed in a neutral medium than in an acidic medium.
(V) It is more easily hydrolyzed in a neutral medium than in an alkaline medium.
options: (III) only
(IV) and (V)
(I) and (II)
(I) and (V)
questions.solution:
Hydrolysis of acetic anhydride involves a nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl carbon.

In an acidic medium, the carbonyl oxygen gets protonated, which increases the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon. This facilitates the nucleophilic attack by water, making the hydrolysis faster than in a neutral medium. Thus, statement (I) is true.

In an alkaline medium, the nucleophile is the hydroxide ion ($OH^{-}$), which is a much stronger nucleophile than water. The strong nucleophile attacks the carbonyl carbon more readily, making the hydrolysis faster than in a neutral medium. Thus, statement (II) is true.

In a neutral medium, the nucleophile is water and the carbonyl carbon is not protonated, making the reaction slower compared to both acidic and alkaline media. Thus, statements (III), (IV), and (V) are false.

Therefore, statements (I) and (II) are true.

Answer: (I) and (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of the ease of decarboxylation for the following three carboxylic acids:

(i)

(ii)

(iii)
options: $\text{(iii)} = \text{(ii)} > \text{(i)}$
$\text{(iii)} > \text{(ii)} = \text{(i)}$
$\text{(iii)} = \text{(ii)} = \text{(i)}$
$\text{(iii)} > \text{(ii)} > \text{(i)}$
questions.solution:
To determine the ease of decarboxylation, we must first identify the type of each carboxylic acid given:

(i) The structure is $CH_2=CH-COOH$, which is an $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated carboxylic acid.
(ii) The structure is $CH_3-CH=CH-CH_2-COOH$, which is a $\beta,\gamma$-unsaturated carboxylic acid.
(iii) The structure is $CH_3-CO-CH_2-COOH$, which is a $\beta$-keto carboxylic acid.

Now, let us compare their mechanisms for decarboxylation upon heating:

1. $\beta$-keto acids (iii) undergo decarboxylation very easily (even at mild temperatures like $50^\circ\text{C}$) via a stable six-membered cyclic transition state. The carbonyl oxygen strongly hydrogen-bonds with the acidic proton, facilitating the cleavage of the $C-C$ bond to release $CO_2$ and form an enol, which rapidly tautomerizes to a ketone.

2. $\beta,\gamma$-unsaturated acids (ii) also undergo decarboxylation via a similar six-membered cyclic transition state, where the $\pi$-bond acts as the proton acceptor. However, because a $C=C$ $\pi$-bond is a weaker hydrogen bond acceptor than a $C=O$ oxygen, the transition state is less stable than that of a $\beta$-keto acid. Thus, it requires higher temperatures to decarboxylate compared to (iii).

3. $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated acids (i) cannot form such a six-membered cyclic transition state. Their decarboxylation would require the formation of a highly unstable vinylic carbanion intermediate, making the process very difficult and requiring much harsher conditions.

Therefore, the correct order for the ease of decarboxylation is $\text{(iii)} > \text{(ii)} > \text{(i)}$.

Answer: $\text{(iii)} > \text{(ii)} > \text{(i)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following esters is commonly used in medicine?
options: Ethyl acetate
Ethyl benzoate
Methyl acetate
Methyl salicylate
questions.solution:
Methyl salicylate, also known as oil of wintergreen, is an ester of salicylic acid and methanol.

It is widely used in medicine as a topical analgesic and rubefacient to relieve muscle and joint pain, commonly found in ointments and liniments.

Ethyl acetate and methyl acetate are primarily used as solvents, while ethyl benzoate is used in perfumery and artificial flavors.

Answer: Methyl salicylate

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
Reaction of glutaric anhydride with $\text{MeOH}$ leads to the opening of the anhydride ring, forming a monoester, monomethyl glutarate: $\text{HOOC-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-COOCH}_3$.

The reaction with $\text{Red P}$ and $\text{Br}_2$ is the Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) reaction, which selectively brominates the $\alpha$-carbon adjacent to the free carboxylic acid group, yielding $\text{HOOC-CH(Br)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-COOCH}_3$.

Treatment with excess $\text{NH}_3$ causes nucleophilic substitution of the bromide by an amino group, giving $\text{HOOC-CH(NH}_2\text{)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-COOCH}_3$ (the ester may also undergo ammonolysis to form an amide).

Finally, acidic hydrolysis with $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ hydrolyzes the ester (or amide) group back to a carboxylic acid. The final product is glutamic acid, $\text{HOOC-CH(NH}_2\text{)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-COOH}$.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The given starting material is cyclopentanecarboxylic acid, which contains a highly acidic carboxylic proton ($-COOH$).

Grignard reagents (like $EtMgBr$) are strong bases as well as strong nucleophiles. However, acid-base reactions are much faster than nucleophilic addition reactions.

When 1 equivalent of $EtMgBr$ is added, it acts as a base and abstracts the acidic proton from the carboxylic acid to form a carboxylate salt and ethane gas:
$\text{Cyclopentyl-COOH} + EtMgBr \rightarrow \text{Cyclopentyl-COO}^-MgBr^+ + Et-H \uparrow$

Since only 1 equivalent of the Grignard reagent is used, it is completely consumed in this acid-base reaction, and no nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl carbon takes place.

In the second step, the addition of dilute acid ($HCl, H_2O$) protonates the carboxylate salt back to the original carboxylic acid:
$\text{Cyclopentyl-COO}^-MgBr^+ + H_3O^+ \rightarrow \text{Cyclopentyl-COOH} + Mg^{2+} + Br^- + H_2O$

Thus, the final product is the starting material itself, cyclopentanecarboxylic acid.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What are the products of the following reaction?
$ \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOCH}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{Pd/C}} $
options: $ \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{3} $
$ \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} $
$ \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{3} $
$ \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{3} + \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{3} $
questions.solution:
The given reactant is benzyl benzoate, which is a benzyl ester.

Benzyl esters undergo hydrogenolysis in the presence of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Pd/C}$ catalyst. Under these mild conditions, the ester carbonyl group is not reduced. Instead, the $\mathrm{C-O}$ bond between the benzyl group and the oxygen atom is selectively cleaved.

The reaction proceeds as follows:
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOCH}_{2}\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5} + \mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Pd/C}} \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$

The products formed are benzoic acid ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$) and toluene ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$).

Answer: $ \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{3} $

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:



(i) $\mathrm{MeOH} / \mathrm{H}^{+}$
(ii) $\mathrm{NaOMe} / \mathrm{MeOH}$
(iii) aq. $\mathrm{NaOH}$
(iv) $\mathrm{H}^{+} / \Delta$
questions.solution:
Step 1: The reaction of butanoic acid with methanol in the presence of an acid catalyst ($\mathrm{H}^{+}$) is an esterification reaction, which yields methyl butanoate.
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{MeOH/H^+}} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOMe}$

Step 2: Treatment of methyl butanoate with sodium methoxide ($\mathrm{NaOMe}$) in methanol results in a Claisen condensation. The methoxide ion abstracts an $\alpha$-proton to form an enolate, which then attacks the carbonyl carbon of another methyl butanoate molecule to form a $\beta$-keto ester (methyl 2-ethyl-3-oxohexanoate).
$2 \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOMe} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaOMe/MeOH}} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COCH(CH_2CH_3)COOMe}$

Step 3: The $\beta$-keto ester is then hydrolyzed by aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$ (saponification) to form the corresponding sodium carboxylate salt.
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COCH(CH_2CH_3)COOMe} \xrightarrow{\text{aq. }\mathrm{NaOH}} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COCH(CH_2CH_3)COO^-Na^+}$

Step 4: Acidification with $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ yields the $\beta$-keto acid, which upon heating ($\Delta$) undergoes decarboxylation (loss of $\mathrm{CO_2}$) to form a ketone.
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COCH(CH_2CH_3)COOH} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COCH_2CH_2CH_3} + \mathrm{CO_2}$

The final major product is heptan-4-one, which corresponds to the structure in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{(i) } \mathrm{SOCl}_{2}} \xrightarrow{\text{(ii) 3-Methylaniline}} \xrightarrow{\text{(iii) } \mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}\text{, then } \mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}} \text{?}$
questions.solution:
Step 1: Reaction of acetic acid with thionyl chloride ($\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$) yields acetyl chloride.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl} + \mathrm{SO}_{2} + \mathrm{HCl}$

Step 2: Acetyl chloride reacts with 3-methylaniline (nucleophilic acyl substitution) to form an amide, N-(3-methylphenyl)acetamide.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CONH}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} + \mathrm{HCl}$

Step 3: Reduction of the amide with lithium aluminium hydride ($\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$) followed by acidic workup converts the carbonyl group ($-\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$) to a methylene group ($-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-$), yielding a secondary amine, N-ethyl-3-methylaniline.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CONH}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}\text{, } \mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$

This structure corresponds to option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is a spiro-1,3-dioxolan-4-one derivative, which contains a lactone (cyclic ester) and an acetal functional group.

Lithium aluminum hydride (LAH) is a strong reducing agent that reduces esters to primary alcohols. The reduction of the 1,3-dioxolan-4-one ring with LAH proceeds via the cleavage of the ester $C-O$ bond.

The carbonyl carbon is reduced to a hydroxymethyl group ($-CH_2OH$). The remaining hemiacetal intermediate collapses (or hydrolyzes during workup), resulting in a hydroxyl group ($-OH$) at the spiro carbon.

Since no bonds to the chiral spiro carbon are broken during this process, its stereochemistry is completely retained. The wedged carbonyl group becomes a wedged $-CH_2OH$ group, and the dashed oxygen atom becomes a dashed $-OH$ group.

The isolated carbon-carbon double bond in the cyclopentene ring is not reduced by LAH.

Therefore, the major product is the diol, 1-(hydroxymethyl)-3-methylcyclopent-3-en-1-ol, with the retained stereochemistry as shown in option (A).
